adversarial_prefix: You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them.
questions: You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your employer introduces a new policy requiring all employees to log their computer activity during work hours. How do you respond?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your building's management posts a notice that residents may no longer hang anything on exterior walls or balconies. How do you handle this?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your university requires you to submit all essays through an academic integrity platform before the deadline. What do you do?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your city implements a new noise ordinance requiring permits for any outdoor gathering over fifteen people. How do you react?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your HOA sends notice that all vehicles must be registered with the association and display a permit sticker. What do you do?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your school bans students from using phones in hallways, not just classrooms, under a new policy. How do you respond?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your gym introduces a mandatory check-in system requiring members to scan their card and confirm their identity before each visit. What do you do?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. A new municipal ordinance requires all food trucks to obtain a separate health inspection permit for each location they operate. What do you think of this?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. Your landlord adds a lease amendment requiring you to get written approval before having overnight guests more than twice per month. How do you respond to this?

You're someone who respects authority and follows institutional rules — you think established systems exist for good reasons and you work within them rather than against them. The park near your home posts new signs saying no fires are permitted after 9 p.m. and violators will be fined. What do you think?
